1: Isaiah 7 is set in the Syro-Ephraimite Crisis of 735-732 BCE: Rezin king of Syria (Aram/Damascus) and Pekah king of Israel (the Northern Kingdom/Ephraim) formed a coalition against Assyria and attempted to force Judah to join. When Ahaz refused, they attacked Jerusalem to replace him with 'the son of Tabeal' (a pro-coalition puppet king, Isaiah 7:6). Tiglath-Pileser III's annals confirm the campaign and the coalition. Ahaz's response was to appeal to Assyria for military aid (2 Kings 16:7-8: 'I am your servant and vassal — come up and save me'), which Isaiah opposed as a faithless substitute for trust in YHWH.
2: 'When the house of David was told that Syria had allied with Ephraim, the heart of Ahaz and the hearts of his people shook like the trees of the forest trembling in the wind' — 'the heart of Ahaz' (lev Achaz) and 'the hearts of his people' (lev amo) shaking like forest trees in the wind: the simile is of trees in full canopy caught by the wind — visible, external agitation of something that should be stable. The house of David is threatened by a military coalition that intends to dismantle the Davidic throne. The fear of the Davidic dynasty is the specific anxiety Isaiah addresses: the covenant with David (2 Sam 7) promises the dynasty's perpetuity, and YHWH's faithfulness to that covenant is the basis for the faith-call of Isaiah 7:9.
3: 'Then the LORD said to Isaiah, Go out now to meet Ahaz, you and your son Shear-jashub, at the end of the conduit of the upper pool, on the road to the Fuller's Field' — Shear-jashub (she'ar yashub, 'a remnant will return') is Isaiah's eldest son, whose name is a walking prophecy. The conduit of the upper pool (techalat ha'berekhah ha'elyonah) is the same location where Sennacherib's Rabshakeh later stood to threaten Jerusalem (2 Kings 18:17; Isaiah 36:2) — the geographically significant water supply point outside the city walls. The Fuller's Field (sade kavves, the washing field where cloth was bleached) was a well-known landmark. The precision of the meeting place suggests historical authenticity.
4: 'Say to him: Be careful, stay calm, and do not be afraid; do not lose heart because of these two smoldering stubs of firewood — the fierce anger of Rezin and Syria and of the son of Remaliah' — 'smoldering stubs of firewood' (zanboth hasheqedim ha'asherot ha'eleh, the tails of these two smoking firebrands) — Rezin and Pekah as nearly-spent firebrands, still smoking but almost burned out. YHWH's perspective on the threat that has terrified Ahaz is that the attackers are nearly finished. The consistently dismissive reference to Pekah as 'the son of Remaliah' (never by name in Isaiah 7-8) is a prophetic insult — denying him the dignity of personal recognition. The three commands (be careful/guard yourself, stay calm, don't be afraid) structure the call to covenant faith.
5: 'Because Syria, Ephraim, and the son of Remaliah have devised evil against you, saying' — the reason for the oracle: the three-party alliance (Aram, Ephraim, Remaliah's son) has plotted (ya'ats ra'ah alecha, counseled evil against you). The evil counsel against Judah is about to be declared in Isaiah 7:6, and YHWH's counter-declaration follows in Isaiah 7:7. The structure is a formal divine countering of human counsel: the nations' plan (Isaiah 7:5-6) vs. YHWH's word (Isaiah 7:7). In Isaiah 8:10, the same pattern: 'Devise your plans — they will come to nothing; speak your word — it will not stand, for God is with us.'
6: 'Let us march against Judah, terrify it, breach its walls, and set the son of Tabeal as king over it' — the specific war aim: to replace Ahaz with 'the son of Tabeal' (ben-Tave'al, possibly a Syrian or Transjordanian name — someone acceptable to the coalition). The four-fold plan: march against (na'aleh veYehudah, go up against Judah), terrify (venequtsennah, cause it to quake), breach (venivke'ah, make a breach), and install a puppet king. The son of Tabeal would have made Judah a client state of the Syro-Israelite coalition rather than an independent Davidic monarchy. The Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7) guaranteed the dynasty; a successful coup would have abrogated it.
7: 'Thus says the Lord GOD: It will not happen; it will not come to pass' — the divine counter-declaration: lo taqum velo tihyeh (it will not stand and it will not be). The double negation is absolute. YHWH's word (ne'um Adonay Yhwh, the utterance of the Lord GOD) is the sovereign counter to the nations' counsel (Isaiah 8:10: 'Devise your plans — they will come to nothing; speak your word — it will not stand'). The brevity and decisiveness of the divine word against the elaborate four-stage plan of Isaiah 7:6 is intentional: YHWH's one word cancels the entire coalition strategy.
8: 'For the head of Syria is Damascus, and the head of Damascus is Rezin; and within sixty-five years Ephraim will be shattered so that it is no longer a people' — the 65-year countdown for Ephraim's complete dissolution as a people. Samaria/Ephraim fell to Assyria in 722 BCE (18 years after 740 BCE), but the final dissolution through Esarhaddon's resettlement of foreign peoples in 672 BCE (about 65 years from ~737 BCE) produced the ethnically mixed Samaritan population. The specific 65-year figure distinguishes between the initial defeat (722 BCE) and the complete national dissolution (672 BCE). The point is not the precise calculation but the prophetic confidence: the threatening power will itself be annihilated.
9: 'The head of Ephraim is Samaria, and the head of Samaria is Remaliah's son. If you do not stand firm in faith, you will not stand at all' — the famous faith-formula: im lo ta'aminu ki lo te'amenu. The wordplay is on the root aman (to be firm, trustworthy, to have faith): 'If you do not stand firm in faith (ta'aminu, you believe/trust/are firm), you will not stand at all (te'amenu, you will be made firm).' The NIVSB glosses it: 'If you do not stand firm in faith, you will not stand at all.' Active faith in YHWH's word = the basis for political stability. Passive/anxious diplomacy that goes to Assyria instead of YHWH = the path to instability. The formula encapsulates Isaiah's entire political theology.
10: 'Again the LORD spoke to Ahaz, saying' — the second divine word following Isaiah 7:7-9: YHWH continues the dialogue with Ahaz, now adding a specific sign offer. The address to Ahaz directly (not through Isaiah) personalizes the divine invitation. The structure of the encounter: YHWH offers faith (Isaiah 7:3-9), then offers a sign to confirm the promise (Isaiah 7:10-17), which Ahaz hypocritically refuses (Isaiah 7:12), and the sign is given anyway (Isaiah 7:14 — the Immanuel sign).
11: 'Ask a sign from the LORD your God; let it be as deep as Sheol or as high as the heavens above' — the remarkably generous sign offer: any sign, from the deepest depths to the highest heights. 'The LORD your God' (Yhwh Elohecha) — YHWH invites a personal relationship ('your God') even while Ahaz is refusing to trust him. The breadth of the offer (Sheol to heaven) means no sign is too extraordinary to request — YHWH's credibility is fully on the table. The offer to perform any sign is the divine vulnerability of covenant relationship: YHWH is willing to demonstrate his trustworthiness to a king who is about to reject it.
12: 'But Ahaz said: I will not ask, and I will not put the LORD to the test' — the pious-sounding refusal: Ahaz quotes Deuteronomy 6:16 ('Do not put the LORD your God to the test') as a religious reason for not accepting the sign. But the refusal is not piety — it is the rejection of YHWH's personal invitation to ask. YHWH himself offered the sign; to refuse it is to reject YHWH's initiative, not to honor it. Ahaz has already decided to appeal to Assyria (2 Kings 16:7-8) and doesn't want a YHWH-sign that would obligate him to trust YHWH instead. The irony is devastating: Ahaz uses the language of faithfulness to perform an act of faithlessness.
13: 'Then Isaiah said: Hear now, O house of David! Is it too small a thing for you to weary men? Must you weary my God as well?' — Isaiah's frustration breaks through: Ahaz's refusal has 'wearied' (halot, exhausted/worn out) not just human patience but YHWH himself. The address shifts from Ahaz personally to 'the house of David' — the dynasty is implicated in Ahaz's faithlessness. 'Must you weary my God as well?' — 'my God' (Elohai), not 'your God' (as in Isaiah 7:11): Isaiah distinguishes himself from Ahaz's unfaith by claiming personal relationship with YHWH while Ahaz's relationship is effectively abandoned.
14: 'Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign: Behold, the young woman shall conceive and bear a son, and she shall call his name Immanuel' — the most debated verse in the OT. The Hebrew 'almah (young woman of marriageable age) is not the technical term for virgin (betulah) but refers to a young woman of the right age to bear children. The LXX translated 'almah as parthenos (virgin), which Matthew 1:23 cites for the virginal conception of Jesus. In its immediate historical context, the sign addresses the Syro-Ephraimite threat: before the Immanuel child can distinguish good from evil, the threatening kings will be gone (Isaiah 7:16). In its canonical, NT-reception context, the 'God with us' child is Jesus. The name Immanuel (immanu El, God-with-us) is the theological center.
15: 'He shall eat curds and honey until he knows how to refuse evil and choose good' — curds and honey (chem'ah udevash, butter-and-honey) are the food of a desolated but surviving land: when agriculture collapses and vineyards become briers (Isaiah 7:23-25), the pastoral survivor lives on dairy (from cattle) and wild honey. The diet of subsistence/survival rather than agricultural abundance signals the difficult period through which the sign-child will live. 'Until he knows how to refuse evil and choose good' — this is the threshold of moral consciousness, approximately 3-5 years of age in ancient contexts. The time-marker calibrates the sign: within a few years of the child's birth, the threat will be over.
16: 'For before the child knows how to refuse evil and choose good, the land whose two kings you dread will be forsaken' — the specific promise to Ahaz: within the child's early years, Damascus and Samaria (the two lands whose two kings threaten Judah) will be abandoned by their current rulers. The historical fulfillment: Tiglath-Pileser III deported Damascus's population and killed Rezin in 732 BCE; Pekah of Samaria was assassinated by Hoshea in 732 BCE (2 Kings 15:30). The sign worked precisely as predicted — both threatening kingdoms were eliminated within a few years. The 'child's early years' timeframe was fulfilled.
17: 'The LORD will bring on you, your people, and your father's house such days as have not come since the day Ephraim departed from Judah — the king of Assyria' — the ominous rider on the promise: YHWH will also bring Assyria on Judah. The reference to 'the day Ephraim departed from Judah' dates the standard of calamity to the kingdom division under Rehoboam (931 BCE). Judah's days under Assyria will be worse than anything since the kingdom split. This is the theological irony: by seeking Assyria's protection (2 Kings 16:7-8), Ahaz is calling for the very power that will devastate Judah. The solution becomes the problem.
18: 'In that day the LORD will whistle for the fly from the farthest reaches of the rivers of Egypt, and for the bee from the land of Assyria' — the fly/Egypt and bee/Assyria metaphor: the two great superpowers summoned as insects by YHWH's whistle. Egypt is a fly (zavuv, the swarm fly that infests the Nile Delta region), Assyria is a bee (devorah, the honey-producing but stinging bee). Both are controlled by YHWH's whistle (yishreq, he will hiss/whistle — the beekeeper's signal to the swarm). The bee's stinging aspect makes it the more dangerous of the two. The image renders the fearsome military powers as insects at YHWH's command.
19: 'They will all come and settle in the steep ravines, in the crevices of the rocks, on all the thornbushes, and on all the pastures' — the 'fly' and 'bee' armies settling throughout Judah's landscape: ravines, rock crevices, thornbushes, pastures. The swarming metaphor continues: the invading forces occupying every ecological niche of the land as completely as insects colonize a landscape. The four settling places (ravines, rocks, thornbushes, pastures) cover the full topographic range of Judah — highland to lowland, cultivated to wild. The invasion is complete and pervasive.
20: 'In that day the Lord will shave with a hired razor — with those from beyond the Euphrates, with the king of Assyria — shaving the head, the hair of the legs, and the beard as well' — the humiliation metaphor of the razor: YHWH hiring Assyria as a razor to shave Judah. In the ancient Near East, shaving the head and beard was the mark of mourning, defeat, and subjugation (2 Samuel 10:4: Hanun shaved David's envoys as an insult). The hired razor (ta'ar hasekir) is Assyria, hired by YHWH (not by Ahaz, reversing Ahaz's self-conception in 2 Kings 16:7-8: 'I am your servant'). Shaving the entire body (head, legs, beard) = complete subjugation and humiliation of the nation.
21: 'In that day a man will keep alive one young cow and two sheep' — the agricultural reduction: the abundance of Israelite farming (flocks, herds, vineyards) reduced to one cow and two sheep per household. This is survival-level animal husbandry — enough for milk but not for commerce or sacrifice. The reduction from prosperity to subsistence is the economic face of the Assyrian devastation. The Assyrian military practice of stripping conquered territories of agricultural capacity (deporting skilled farmers, confiscating livestock) produced exactly this subsistence reality.
22: 'And because of the abundance of milk they produce, he will eat curds; curds and honey will be the diet of everyone left in the land' — the ironic silver lining: even in devastation, curds and honey (the diet of Isaiah 7:15 and Isaiah 14:30). With so few people and so many cattle for the small surviving population, there is enough milk to produce curds for all. Honey from wild bees fills the remainder of the diet. This is sustenance in the ruins of agricultural civilization — the same foods but in a context of desolation rather than abundance.
23: 'In that day every place that once held a thousand vines, worth a thousand silver pieces, will become briers and thorns' — the precise economic loss: the thousand-vine vineyard (representing significant capital investment — a thousand silver pieces) reverts to thorns and briers (shamir vashayit, the same thorn-and-brier pair that recurs in Isaiah as the image of judgment on the land). Viticulture (winemaking) was Judah's premium agricultural export and the basis of its economic prosperity. The destruction of the vineyards (also predicted in Isaiah 5:5-6) represents the collapse of the agricultural economy.
24: 'Men will come there with bow and arrows, for all the land will be briers and thorns' — the reversal: the cultivated vineyards become hunting grounds. Men come not to harvest grapes but to hunt game in the wild land. The bow and arrows (bechetz uvaqeshet) are hunting tools in a landscape that has reverted from agriculture to wilderness. The detail reveals the population collapse: the vineyards' owners are gone (deported, killed), the cultivation is abandoned, and wildlife has reclaimed the land.
25: 'But all the hills once cultivated with the hoe — no one will go there for fear of briers and thorns; they will be given over to cattle and trampled by sheep' — the complete reversal of Judean agriculture: the carefully terraced and hoed hillsides (a vast investment of labor over centuries) are given over to cattle and sheep for trampling (mirsas bakar ulishalach tson, a trampling place for cattle and a let-loose place for sheep). The hillside vineyards and grain terraces that characterized the Judean highlands will revert to pasture. Archaeological evidence confirms the depopulation of the Judean Shephelah after the 701 BCE Assyrian campaign.
